title_en: Zakat, Fasting, and Hajj in the Maliki Tradition
title_ar: الزكاة والصيام والحج في المذهب المالكي
content_en:
Ad-Dardiri's treatment of the remaining pillars of Islam in Al-Sharh al-Kabir — zakat, fasting, and hajj — reflects the Maliki school's careful balance between textual fidelity and attention to the lived practice of the Muslim community.

Zakat in the Maliki school follows the same general categories as the other schools — gold and silver, trade goods, livestock, and agricultural produce — with several distinctive positions regarding thresholds, rates, and conditions. The nisab for gold is approximately 20 dinars (roughly 85 grams) and for silver 200 dirhams (approximately 595 grams). The zakat rate on these monetary holdings is 2.5%, payable after one lunar year of ownership (hawl).

The Maliki school has a distinctive ruling regarding zakat on debts: a creditor who is owed money that he is confident of recovering must include that debt in his nisab calculation and pay zakat on it, according to the dominant Maliki position. This contrasts with some other schools' positions on the treatment of receivable debts.

For agricultural produce, the Maliki school limits obligatory zakat to grains and dried fruits (tamr and zabib), consistent with the schools of Shafi'i and Hanbali in broad terms, though with different thresholds and applications. Produce watered naturally (by rain or rivers) is taxed at 10%; produce requiring irrigation at 5%. The Maliki school additionally requires that the produce reach the minimum nisab before zakat is due.

The Maliki school's rulings on zakat al-fitr (the obligatory charity at the end of Ramadan) are detailed in Al-Sharh al-Kabir. It is obligatory on every free Muslim who possesses more than his own needs and the needs of his dependents on the day and night of Eid. It is paid in food (the local staple grain) at the rate of one sa' per person, and must be paid before the Eid prayer — paying it after is still valid but constitutes a lapse of proper observance.

Fasting (sawm) in the Maliki school requires intention formed the night before for each day of Ramadan — a stricter position than some other schools, which permit a single intention at the beginning of Ramadan to cover the entire month. This is based on the Maliki interpretation of the prophetic statement regarding intention. Ad-Dardiri discusses this ruling in Al-Sharh al-Kabir with reference to both the hadith evidence and the positions of major Maliki authorities.

The Maliki school holds that vomiting involuntarily does not break the fast — aligning with the other schools on this point. However, the Maliki school holds that applying kuhl (kohl) to the eyes does not break the fast even if some taste is perceived in the throat, while some other schools treat this with more caution. Ad-Dardiri clarifies the Maliki position and the reasoning behind it.

For hajj, the Maliki school identifies the obligatory acts (fara'id): ihram, standing at Arafah, tawaf al-ifadah, and — distinctively — the sa'y between Safa and Marwa, which the Maliki school considers an obligatory element (rukn) rather than merely a required act (wajib). The Maliki school also holds that shaving or cutting the hair (tahallul) is an obligatory act whose omission requires expiation. Ad-Dardiri's exposition of hajj law in Al-Sharh al-Kabir is detailed and includes discussion of the different states of ihram (ifrad, tamattu', and qiran) and the distinctive Maliki rulings on each.
